Adventure #5—Lights... Camera... “Are you the box man?” my young, female colleague asked, approaching the table where Tom and I were sitting.Change the Action
Adventure #6—The Write Stuff
“I suppose I am,” Tom replied graciously.
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled
Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
I quickly introduced them. “Tom, this is Linda. She handles all of our advertising. Linda, this is Tom, the inventor I	
Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas told you about.” Tom stood up and shook her hand firmly.
Adventure #9—The Beginning
Postscript... Four Years Later “I hear you’ve been helping our boss come up with some amazing ideas to keep the bookstore going,” said
Linda, pulling up a chair from a nearby table.
“ He’s the one who has been doing all the heavy lifting,” Tom corrected her with a friendly smile. “I’ve just been
showing him a new way to get his brain going.”
“Well, I sure could use your help getting mine going. I’ve heard about some of the idea boxes you’ve been
teaching our boss. Now I’m supposed to come up with an idea for this big sale we’re having, and I can’t seem to
think of anything unique.”
“That sounds like a fun one!” said Tom, with far more enthusiasm than I’m usually able to muster for such a task.
“What have you got so far?”
Linda sheepishly showed him a half-page ad with the headline, “Huge Book Sale,” followed by the date and all
the usual information.
“That certainly says what they need to know,” Tom nodded slowly.
“It’s what we always use,” she said with a shrug. “It’s so… expected . I know it can be better. I’m just not sure
where to take it.”
“Well, then let’s take a look in some boxes,” said Tom. “I do a little advertising myself when I market some of my
own inventions. I usually start out in the Change the Interaction’s Intensity box, since advertising is, after all, an
interaction between the advertiser and the prospect.
“In engineering, we change the intensity by adding or removing elements or by changing the environment. On a
grill, pouring gasoline on a fire certainly changes the intensity of the interaction between the charcoal and the
fire, doesn’t it? Then, closing the lid changes the environment by removing oxygen and this changes the
interaction in the opposite way.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 7 --

“Once, the city’s Department of Waterworks called me because they wanted to speed up the water purification
process. They were using bacteria to clean the water, and I was able to speed things up by improving the
interaction between the bacteria and the water. We couldn’t increase the number or type of bacteria and we
couldn’t use bigger tanks. Care to guess how we did it?”
“Oxygen?” came Linda’s high-pitched guess.
“Right!” Tom pointed at her excitedly. “You got it! Pumping air through the water energized those bacteria and
gave them quite an appetite. How did you guess?”
“I took some biology courses in college,” she replied sheepishly.
“Great! Then let’s put that scientific mind to work on your advertising,” said Tom. “Can you think of a way to
change the interactivity of your ad to make it more interactive and engaging?”
Linda thought for a moment. “What about a question? That’s interactive and engaging. Maybe something like:
‘Do You Like to Buy Books
But Dislike High Prices?’”
“Good,” said Tom. “That might get my attention. Now, can we throw a little more gasoline on the fire and increase
the interaction even further? Some emotion , perhaps?”
I couldn’t resist. I blurted, “What about:
‘Do You Love to Buy Books
But Hate to Pay High Prices?’”
“Good,” said Tom quickly. “There’s nothing like some emotion-laden words to increase the intensity. Any other
ways to add fuel to the fire?”
Linda suggested: “What about a warning? That would stir things up. Something like:
‘Don’t Buy Another Book
Until You Read This Ad.’”
“Good!” said Tom again. “You can also use surprise, curiosity, flattery—anything that will increase that interaction
between you and the reader.
“Now, instead of adding fuel, why not try changing the interaction by making it less intense? In advertising today,
it seems to me that there’s so much shouting going on that sometimes going in the opposite direction and
calming things down can really stand out. Kind of like how kindergarten teachers use a soft voice instead of
screaming to get their pupils’ attention.”
Linda scrunched up her face and thought for a while. “What about just calmly saying something like:
‘A Very Special Invitation to
Every Area Booklover
Who Would Like to
Read More and Pay Less.’”
“Wow,” I said, “that’s so understated—yet powerful at the same time.”
“Ready for the next interaction box?” queried Tom.
“Tom,” I interrupted. “I’ve noticed that you usually go on to something new just when we come to a really good
idea that I’d like to spend more time with.”
“You’re exactly right,” nodded Tom. “It’s important, when generating ideas to really generate ideas. You want to
keep moving. It’s just as dangerous to get ‘stuck’ on a good idea as on a bad one. There’ll always be plenty of
time for expanding on any idea later on.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 7 --

“Then let’s move ,” I said eagerly.
“The next ‘interaction’ box is one I call Add Something to the Interaction . One way you can do this is by adding
an intermediary of some kind.
“A technical example would be how, for centuries, people applied paint with brushes—a labor-intensive
process, as you probably know. Then, one day, someone thought to add an intermediary to the process—air!—
and spray paint was born.
“Another way to add something to an interaction is by adding an element of feedback. Add a small round hole,
and the kettle whistles when the water starts to boil. And how many lives have been saved because someone
thought to design smoke detectors that beep at you when the battery gets low? Now, Linda—start
supplementing the interaction!”
Linda launched right in. “Intermediary… Well, we could ask a local celebrity to appear in the ad—a well-known
author who might be willing to say something about why people should come to our sale?”
“I know plenty of local authors who would be willing to do that,” I said, nodding along. “ They sure want to see this
bookstore succeed.”
“Good,” said Tom. “Now, feedback.”
“What if we put a coupon in the ad?” suggested Linda, “that asked people to vote on what books they would like
to see go on sale? Not only would that be fun, but it would give us valuable feedback on the books people are
really interested in. It would also be a good way to get people involved , or to ‘intensify the interaction,’ as you
say.”
“You’re really getting the hang of this, Linda,” said Tom. “I assume your boss has told you about the boxes we’ve
talked about so far and the importance of coming up with a lot of ideas quickly.”
“He has.”
“Good. Then why don’t you two come up with some more ideas as quickly as you can, as I take you through
some of the boxes I like to use for marketing?”
I looked at Linda, who was already nodding. I smiled and nodded along too.
“Great,” said Tom. “Are you both ready?”
“Ready,” we said in unison.
“ Add a New Dimension ,” began Tom. “Perhaps there’s some aspect of the sale you can highlight or a different
way of looking at it.”
“What about the dimension of quantity?” offered Linda. “We could highlight the number of books going on sale:
‘2,207 Books That Will
Never Be Priced This Low Again.’”
I jumped in: “Or, we could highlight the savings:
‘20% to 50% Off
Every Book in the Store.’”
“Then, there’s the ‘time’ dimension,” added Linda:
“‘Why This Sunday Will Be
the Best Day in Years
to Get the Books You Want.’”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 7 --

“And,” I added, “since a ‘new dimension’ could also be a different way of looking at it, instead of calling it a
‘book sale,’ we could call it a ‘Build Your Library for Less’ sale.”
“Or,” said Linda, “we could add a new dimension by relating it to something else, such as saying, ‘It’s the next
best thing to having an uncle in the book business.’ Gosh, this is fun,” she chuckled.
“Great. Now Divide It Up ,” said Tom, moving us right along.
Linda, again: “Instead of featuring just a single book, we could divide the ad into a grid and show many different
books with their prices.”
“ Bring Some Things Together ,” said Tom, quickening the pace. “This is wide open. What can you combine with
the headline or bring into the ad that would make it more interesting or attention-getting?”
I suggested, “We could tap into something in the news:
‘Want to Understand
What’s Going on Between Ukraine and Russia?
Now You Can. For Less.’”
“ Do It Beforehand .”
“We could send out postcards,” suggested Linda, “or… make phone calls, telling people to look for the big ad in
the paper this Sunday. Or even inviting them personally.”
“Better yet,” I added, “what if, before the main sale, we had a special sale for our regulars?”
“And, for the grand finale,” announced Tom, “ Do the Opposite .”
“The opposite…” mused Linda, biting her lip. “What could that be? Let’s see… the opposite of selling books
is… giving them away?”
“Mmm… This one’s making me nervous,” I joked.
“So,” she continued, “how do we make it work that we give books away? What if, instead of a 20 percent
discount, we just gave away every fifth book for free? That’s mathematically the same, isn’t it?”
“Well, yes,” I agreed, shrugging. “If the book doesn’t cost any more than the average price of the other four
books.”
“We can make some kind of stipulation,” said Linda, “but the important thing is that it would enable us to
advertise that we’re giving away ‘free books.’ And people might be tempted to buy more books than they
otherwise would in order to get that fifth book without having to pay for it.”
“They just might,” I agreed.
Linda jumped up suddenly. “My brain can’t handle any more ideas!” she exclaimed. “I’m going to go take the
best of all this and write an ad that’s gonna raise a lot of eyebrows.”
And she turned on her heel and practically skipped away.
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
Ah, the amazing power of words!
Words are the driving force behind all relationships—businesses and personal.
Words convey ideas, instructions and information.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 7 --

Words persuade .
And every business depends on words to promote its goods or services to prospective customers.
But in fact all 15 of these boxes can be applied to any type of writing or speaking. They can help you determine
what you need to say, as well as the best way to say it. Writing is a matter of choices—choosing the best way to
say something and the best words to help you express it. And these boxes help you generate more and better
choices.
Since writing or speaking is basically an interaction between you and the reader (or listener), it makes sense
that the specific boxes we looked at in this Adventure were the two “Interaction” boxes.
Let’s play for a while in these boxes…
Click here to download this fillable PDF
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 7 --

Click here to download this fillable PDF
MODULES
Your Action Steps
Whenever you have to present or write anything, use as many of the idea-generating boxes as you can to
see if there might be a better way to say or write it. Try using different boxes each time. Or go through them
all quickly and see if any jump out at you as being especially appropriate.
Become more aware of interactions. At home. At the office. With others. Among others. Inside yourself.
Between you and ideas. In your writing. Everywhere! And always look for opportunities to change or add
something to these interactions.
And now, let’s tackle idea generation for the increasingly omnipresent online world…
When brainstorming and generating ideas, don’t dwell on good ideas or bad ones. Keep
moving. There’s plenty of time for elaboration and evaluation later.
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
